name: The Day the Stone Breathed Back
provenance: Told in UTTP · Travels as a confession whispered over cheap ale
summary: A surface soldier's account of the morning the divine went quiet and the enemy stopped hating itself.
content:
I still reach for it sometimes. Twenty years later, middle of the night, some old soldier reflex — hand goes to the holy symbol, waiting for that warmth. And nothing comes. Hasn't come since the Black Ribs.

I was with the 4th Sun-Guard when we pushed into the vein near what they call Vragathul now. Every tactical manual said the same thing: hold your line, wait for the Duergar shields to buckle, let the Drow stab them from the dark, then roll through whatever's left. Three hundred years that worked. Three hundred years we counted on their gods hating each other more than they hated us.

That morning, the line didn't buckle.

I saw the Duergar shields — thick and stubborn as the mountain — and through the gaps, Drow shadow-weavers were casting. Not competing. *Anchoring.* The Duergar were the anvil. The Drow were the silk-cut. I raised my holy symbol and waited for the Light, that familiar warmth that meant something was listening, something was with us. What I felt instead was the weight of how far away the sky was. How long it had been quiet. How long we had been fighting a war with gods who'd already left the room.

They hadn't just stopped talking. They'd left us in the dark with an enemy we'd spent three centuries teaching how to hate us. And the enemy had finally decided that surviving us was worth more than pleasing a god who wasn't answering prayers anyway.

I've never told a superior officer what I saw that day. I don't think it would help.
